title: What is an array
instruction:
Imagine a library shelf with exactly 5 numbered slots: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4.

Every slot holds exactly one book. The number of slots never changes. All slots are the same size.

An array is that shelf — a fixed number of slots, all the same type, each with a number.

Run this and see five slots, all starting at zero.
successMessage: Five slots, all zero. Go fills every slot with the zero value of the type when you declare an array without values.

title: Declare an array with values
instruction:
You can fill a shelf with books right when you create it.

`books := [3]string{"Dune", "Neuromancer", "Snow Crash"}`

The number in brackets is the size. It must match exactly how many values you provide.

Change the three book titles to your own favorites and run it.
hint: Replace the three book titles inside the curly braces with your own. Keep the size [3] and the quotes.

codeChecks.message: Keep the [3]string format and provide three string values in curly braces.
Change the book titles to your own choices.

successMessage: Size in brackets. Type after. Values inside curly braces. That is the full declaration syntax.

title: Access by index
instruction:
Shelf slots are numbered starting at 0 — not 1.

So for a shelf of size 3:
- `books[0]` is the first book
- `books[1]` is the second
- `books[2]` is the third — the last one

This trips up almost everyone at first. There is no slot 3 in a 3-slot shelf.

Print each book title on its own line using its index.
hint: fmt.Println(books[0]), fmt.Println(books[1]), fmt.Println(books[2])  — three separate lines.

codeChecks.message: Access the first book with books[0]
Access the second book with books[1]
Access the third book with books[2]
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Indexes start at 0. Always. In every programming language. You will get used to it.

title: Update a value
instruction:
You can change any slot after the shelf is created. Use the same index syntax — but with `=` instead of just reading.

`shelf[2] = 99`

This program prints the shelf before and after an update. Change slot `[1]` from its current value to `88` and run it.
hint: shelf[1] = 88  — add this line between the two Println calls.

codeChecks.message: Update slot 1: shelf[1] = 88
Replace the TODO comment with your assignment.

successMessage: Read with brackets. Write with brackets and equals. The slot changes, everything else stays.

title: The length of an array
instruction:
`len()` tells you how many slots a shelf has.

This sounds obvious when you wrote the shelf yourself. But it matters when you are working with shelves you did not create — coming from a function, a file, a database.

Run this. Then add a line that prints the length of the `books` shelf.
hint: fmt.Println(len(books))  — len() works on any array.

codeChecks.message: Use len(books) to get the length of the books array.
Replace the TODO comment with your Println.

successMessage: len() is how you ask an array how big it is. You will use this constantly — especially inside loops.

title: Loop through an array
instruction:
Now loops and shelves click together.

Instead of printing `shelf[0]`, `shelf[1]`, `shelf[2]`... one by one, use a loop with `len()` to print every element automatically — no matter how big the shelf gets.

Add the loop to print all five shelf values.
hint: for i := 0; i < len(shelf); i++ { fmt.Println(shelf[i]) }

codeChecks.message: Start your loop at i := 0 since arrays are zero-indexed.
Use len(shelf) as the loop condition.
Access each element using shelf[i] inside the loop.
Replace the TODO comment with your loop.

successMessage: One loop, any size. This pattern — loop from 0 to len — is one of the most common things you will write in Go.

title: range — the cleaner loop
instruction:
`for i, v := range shelf` is a cleaner way to loop.

- `i` is the index (0, 1, 2...)
- `v` is the value at that index

No manual counter. No `len()`. Go handles it.

Change the existing loop to use `range` instead.
hint: for i, v := range books { fmt.Println(i, v) }  — replace the entire for loop.

codeChecks.message: Use range to loop: for i, v := range books
Replace the old loop with a range loop.

successMessage: range gives you the index and value in one shot. Cleaner, less error-prone, more Go-like.

title: The blank identifier _
instruction:
Sometimes you only want the value from `range`, not the index.

But Go requires you to use every variable you declare. If you declare `i` and never use it, Go refuses to compile.

The fix: use `_` (blank identifier) in place of the index. It tells Go: I know this exists, I am intentionally ignoring it.

`for _, v := range books`

Change the loop to use `_` and only print the book titles without the index number.
hint: for _, book := range books { fmt.Println(book) }  — _ discards the index.

codeChecks.message: Use _ to discard the index: for _, v := range books
Remove the index from the Println — only print the value.

successMessage: _ is Go's way of saying: I know there is something here, I just do not need it. You will see it everywhere.

title: Multi-dimensional array
instruction:
A shelf can hold other shelves. This creates a grid — rows and columns.

`var bookcase [3][3]int` is a 3x3 bookcase of shelf slots.

Access a cell with two indexes: `bookcase[row][column]`

Run this. Then change the value at row 1, column 2 to `99`.
hint: bookcase[1][2] = 99  — add this line before the loop.

codeChecks.message: Set bookcase[1][2] = 99 before the loop.
Replace the TODO comment with your assignment.

successMessage: Row first, column second. bookcase[1][2] is row 1, column 2. Spreadsheets, game boards, image pixels — all multi-dimensional arrays.

title: Arrays are fixed size
instruction:
This is the one big limitation of arrays: the size is set when you create them and cannot change.

This program tries to create a shelf with 3 slots but give it 5 books.

Run it. Read the error. Then fix it by either changing the size to 5 or removing the extra books.
hint: Change [3]string to [5]string to fit all five book titles.
expectedOutput: [Dune Neuromancer Snow Crash Hyperion Altered Carbon]

codeChecks.message: Change [3]string to [5]string to fit all five values.

successMessage: You cannot stretch an array. That rigidness is a feature — it guarantees the size is always known. When you need a list that can grow, that is what slices are for. Next chapter.

title: Find the thickest book
instruction:
A classic loop pattern: go through every element and keep track of the largest one seen so far.

Start by assuming the first book (by page count) is the thickest. Then check each subsequent book — if it is thicker, update your record.

Add the loop to find and print the highest page count.
hint: Start with thickest := pages[0]. Then loop from index 1. If pages[i] > thickest, update thickest. Print thickest after the loop.

codeChecks.message: Start with thickest := pages[0] as your initial best guess.
Compare each element: if pages[i] > thickest, update thickest.
Print thickest after the loop ends.
Replace the TODO comment with your loop.

successMessage: Scanning for a max value is one of the most common things programs do. Search engines, leaderboards, analytics — all built on this pattern.

title: Build a reading list
instruction:
No starter code.

Create two arrays of size 5:
- `titles` — five book titles (strings)
- `pages`  — five page counts (ints)

Then loop through both at the same time using the index, and print each line as:
`"1. Dune - 412"`

The number at the start is the rank (index + 1).
hint: for i := 0; i < len(titles); i++ { fmt.Printf("%d. %s - %d\n", i+1, titles[i], pages[i]) }

codeChecks.message: Create a titles array of type [5]string.
Create a pages array of type [5]int.
Access book titles using titles[i] inside the loop.
Access pages using pages[i] inside the loop.
Use i+1 for the rank number so it starts at 1 not 0.

successMessage: A reading list from scratch. Two arrays, one loop, formatted output. That is a real feature in a real app.
